topics.name: Mathematical Methods
topics.examGuidance: Use formal mathematical notation and VCE command verbs. For tech-free: require exact arithmetic and manual algebraic manipulation (no technology). For tech-active: allow CAS for complex numerical work, rounded as specified. Confine questions to the VCE Units 3 and 4 Study Design. State domains for functions and give exact coordinates for key graphical features; include constants of integration where required. Avoid explicitly stating certain techniques, such as the 'product rule' — instead, design questions that require them to choose and apply appropriate methods themselves.

subtopics.name: Function Notation and Domains
subtopics.technique_notes: Create questions requiring students to define functions in the form $f: \text{dom} \to \mathbb{R}, f(x) = \dots$, identify maximal, implied, and natural domains, and apply domain restrictions. Require explicit domain statements for every rule. Include tasks where students determine whether a given value lies in the domain and justify using inequality or set notation. Write all functions as $f: \text{domain} \to \mathbb{R}, f(x) = \dots$ — never omit the domain specification. Express domains using interval notation (e.g. $[a,b)$, $(-\infty, a)$) or set notation (e.g. $\{x \in \mathbb{R} : x > 0\}$). For tech-free items ensure domain conditions are algebraically resolvable by hand.

subtopics.name: Key Graph Features
subtopics.technique_notes: Design questions where students identify and calculate exact coordinates of x- and y-intercepts, stationary points, and equations of horizontal, vertical, and oblique asymptotes for polynomial, rational, exponential, logarithmic, and circular functions. Require algebraic justification for all features, not graphical reading. Require exact coordinates (fractions, surds, $\pi$, $\log_e$ expressions) — no decimal approximations for tech-free items. For asymptotes, state equations in the form $x = a$ or $y = b$. Always verify stationary points using a gradient table or second derivative test.

subtopics.name: Sketching and Interval Notation
subtopics.technique_notes: Create multi-part questions where students use identified key features (intercepts, stationary points, asymptotes, end behaviour) to produce a fully labelled sketch, then state the domain and range using both interval and set notation. Include questions that require students to read domain or range from a given sketch. Insist on labelling all key features on sketches: intercepts with exact coordinates, asymptote equations, and stationary point coordinates. Express ranges using the same interval or set notation conventions as domains. For piecewise functions, consider domain and range of each branch separately before combining.

subtopics.name: Composite Functions
subtopics.technique_notes: Construct questions requiring students to form $(f \circ g)(x)$, verify that $\mathrm{ran}(g) \subseteq \mathrm{dom}(f)$ before proceeding, and determine the domain and range of the composition. Include cases where the composition is not defined and students must identify why. Progress from straightforward compositions to cases requiring domain restriction of $g$. Always verify $\mathrm{ran}(g) \subseteq \mathrm{dom}(f)$ explicitly before forming the composition — show this step. State the domain of $f \circ g$ as $\{x \in \mathrm{dom}(g) : g(x) \in \mathrm{dom}(f)\}$. For tech-free items, keep the algebra of substitution manageable by hand.

subtopics.name: Inverse Functions
subtopics.technique_notes: Develop questions where students determine whether a function has an inverse (one-to-one condition), find $f^{-1}$ algebraically by swapping $x$ and $y$ and solving, state the domain and range of $f^{-1}$, and sketch both $f$ and $f^{-1}$ on the same axes showing reflection in $y = x$. Include cases requiring domain restriction to make $f$ one-to-one. State inverse functions in the form $f^{-1}: \mathrm{dom}(f^{-1}) \to \mathbb{R}, f^{-1}(x) = \dots$ — always include the domain. Verify using $f(f^{-1}(x)) = x$ and $f^{-1}(f(x)) = x$ where appropriate. On sketches, draw $y = x$ as a dashed reference line.

subtopics.name: Piecewise and Hybrid Functions
subtopics.technique_notes: Create questions involving piecewise (hybrid) functions where students must: state the rule and domain for each piece, determine whether the function is continuous at boundary points (using left/right limit comparison), sketch the complete function, and determine the overall domain and range. Include modelling contexts where a hybrid function arises naturally. **Always use the LaTeX `\begin{cases}...\end{cases}` environment for piecewise functions.** The format is: `$f(x) = \begin{cases} \text{rule 1} & \text{if condition 1} \\ \text{rule 2} & \text{if condition 2} \\ \vdots \end{cases}$`. Example: `$f(x) = \begin{cases} -1 & \text{if } x \leq -1 \\ x & \text{if } x \in [-1, 1] \\ 1 & \text{if } x \geq 1 \end{cases}$`. For continuity checks at $x = a$, write $\lim_{x \to a^-} f(x)$, $\lim_{x \to a^+} f(x)$, and $f(a)$ explicitly. Use open/closed dots on sketches to indicate whether endpoints are included.

subtopics.name: Dilations
subtopics.technique_notes: Generate questions requiring students to apply and identify vertical dilations (from the x-axis, factor $|a|$) and horizontal dilations (from the y-axis, factor $1/|b|$) to standard functions. Include tasks to write the transformed equation, state the dilation in standard language, and track how key coordinates change. Test the common confusion between dilation factor and its reciprocal for horizontal dilations. Use standard language: 'dilation by factor $|a|$ from the x-axis' (vertical) and 'dilation by factor $1/|b|$ from the y-axis' (horizontal). Always track the mapping $(x, y) \to (x', y')$ to verify transformed coordinates. For tech-free items, use integer or simple rational dilation factors.

subtopics.name: Reflections and Translations
subtopics.technique_notes: Create questions where students apply reflections across the x-axis ($y \to -y$) or y-axis ($x \to -x$), and horizontal/vertical translations, individually and in combination. Emphasise sign conventions: a translation of $h$ units right corresponds to replacing $x$ with $x - h$ (not $x + h$). Include tasks identifying the transformation from two given equations. Use the general form $y = af(b(x-h))+k$ to identify all parameters. Describe each transformation in plain language with a stated direction and magnitude before writing the algebraic form. For combined transformations, number each step in the correct sequence (dilations and reflections before translations unless otherwise indicated).

subtopics.name: Identifying Parameters from Graphs
subtopics.technique_notes: Present students with sketches of transformed functions and require them to read off or calculate the parameters $a$, $b$, $h$, and $k$ in $y = af(b(x-h))+k$. Include cases involving amplitude and period for circular functions, and asymptote shifts for exponential and logarithmic functions. Require written justification linking graph features to parameter values. Structure these questions by first asking students to identify the base function, then determine each parameter in order: $a$, $b$, $h$, $k$. Require a written sentence linking each graph feature (e.g. 'the period is $\pi$, so $2\pi/b = \pi$, giving $b = 2$') to the parameter value.

subtopics.name: Coordinate Mapping
subtopics.technique_notes: Develop questions where students systematically track how specific points or features are transformed through a sequence of operations, using the general mapping $(x, y) \to (x/b + h,\, ay + k)$. Include multi-step sequences and require students to verify that the image coordinates lie on the transformed curve. Test reverse mapping — finding the original point given the image. Set up coordinate mapping as a table: original point $(x, y)$, mapping rule, image point $(x', y')$. Require at least 3 key points to be tracked per transformation sequence. For reverse problems, solve the mapping equations algebraically and verify by substituting back.

subtopics.name: Families of Transformed Functions
subtopics.technique_notes: Create questions where a single parameter (e.g. $a$, $b$, or $k$) varies continuously and students describe the resulting family of curves — how key features (intercepts, asymptotes, turning points, range) change with the parameter. Include tasks requiring students to find the value of the parameter satisfying a given condition (e.g. the graph passes through a specific point or the range equals a specific interval). For family questions, express the answer as a condition on the parameter (e.g. '$a > 0$' or '$k < -3$') rather than a single value when the question asks for a set of values. For 'find the parameter' questions, substitute the given constraint and solve algebraically.

subtopics.name: Solving Polynomial Equations
subtopics.technique_notes: Construct questions requiring students to solve polynomial equations of degree $n$, understanding that up to $n$ real solutions may exist. Include factorisation over the rationals, identification of repeated roots, and use of the null factor law. Require students to verify solutions and state all roots with multiplicity. Include both exact (tech-free) and numerical problem types. For tech-free items, restrict to polynomials that factorise over the rationals with integer or simple fractional roots. Always write solutions using the null factor law explicitly: 'if $(x-a)(x-b) = 0$ then $x = a$ or $x = b$'. State repeated roots with their multiplicity.

subtopics.name: Inverse and Exponential/Log Equations
subtopics.technique_notes: Design problems where students solve complex equations by applying inverse operations — using $\log_e$ to solve exponential equations and applying $e^x$ to solve logarithmic equations. Extend to power equations solved with fractional exponents and circular equations solved using $\sin^{-1}$, $\cos^{-1}$, $\tan^{-1}$ with appropriate domain restrictions. Require verification of solutions against domain constraints. Show each inverse operation step explicitly. For exponential equations, take $\log_e$ of both sides; for logarithmic equations, exponentiate both sides. Always verify that solutions satisfy all domain constraints — particularly $f(x) > 0$ for logarithmic arguments. State the final answer in exact form using $\log_e$ notation.

subtopics.name: Literal Equations and Parametric Systems
subtopics.technique_notes: Develop algebra questions requiring students to rearrange literal equations to express one variable in terms of others (e.g. solve for $x$ in terms of $a$ and $b$). Also include simultaneous linear systems containing a parameter, where students solve using substitution or elimination and express the general solution in terms of the parameter. Include tasks to determine parameter values giving no solution, a unique solution, or infinitely many solutions. For literal equations, clearly identify the target variable and show each rearrangement step. For parametric simultaneous systems, form the matrix equation $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ and compute $\Delta = ad - bc$ to classify. When $\Delta = 0$, substitute back to determine whether the system is inconsistent or dependent.

subtopics.name: Matrix Methods for Linear Systems
subtopics.technique_notes: Create questions where students represent simultaneous linear equations as a matrix equation $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$, compute the determinant $\Delta = ad - bc$ to classify the solution type, and solve using the inverse matrix method when $\Delta \neq 0$. Include geometric interpretation tasks linking the solution type to the relative position of the corresponding lines. Always write the system in matrix form before computing the determinant. State the classification ('unique solution', 'no solution', or 'infinitely many solutions') before solving. For the unique solution case, show the inverse matrix and multiplication in full. For the degenerate case, test consistency by substituting one equation into the other.

subtopics.name: Domain Constraints in Composition and Inverse Contexts
subtopics.technique_notes: Construct targeted questions requiring rigorous domain specification whenever functions are composed or inverted. Students must state the restricted domain of a function needed to guarantee its inverse exists, verify that a composition is defined by checking range-domain compatibility, and express the domain of the result using correct notation. Include multi-function chains requiring step-by-step domain analysis. State domain constraints at every step — never carry an undefined expression forward. For composite chains $f \circ g \circ h$, work from the inside out: determine $\mathrm{ran}(h)$, check it is a subset of $\mathrm{dom}(g)$, determine $\mathrm{ran}(g|_{\text{restricted}})$, then check against $\mathrm{dom}(f)$. Use interval notation throughout.

subtopics.name: Amplitude, Period, and Phase Shift
subtopics.technique_notes: Generate questions where students extract amplitude $|a|$, period ($2\pi/b$ for sine/cosine or $\pi/b$ for tangent), and phase shift $h$ from equations of the form $y = a\sin(b(x-h))+k$ or equivalents. Include reverse tasks: constructing the equation given amplitude, period, phase, and vertical shift. Test the distinction between $y = a\sin(bx + c)$ and $y = a\sin(b(x - h))$ forms and conversion between them. Always factor the coefficient of $x$ before reading the phase shift: rewrite $a\sin(bx + c)$ as $a\sin(b(x + c/b))$ to identify $h = -c/b$. Derive period algebraically from $b$, not by reading a graph. Express answers in exact form (fractions of $\pi$).

subtopics.name: Exact Values and CAST Reasoning
subtopics.technique_notes: Create problems requiring students to solve circular function equations (e.g. $\sin x = \sqrt{3}/2$) over specified intervals, listing all exact solutions using periodicity and CAST quadrant reasoning. Require students to recall exact values for reference angles $0, \pi/6, \pi/4, \pi/3, \pi/2$ and apply symmetry to generate all solutions. Include both positive and negative values of the trigonometric function. Structure solutions as: (1) identify the reference angle using the exact value, (2) apply CAST to find all solutions in $[0, 2\pi)$ or $[0, \pi)$ for tan, (3) apply periodicity to extend to the given interval. Express all answers in exact radian form. For equations like $\sin(2x) = k$, substitute $u = 2x$, solve for $u$ over the expanded interval, then divide by 2.

subtopics.name: Graphing Circular Functions
subtopics.technique_notes: Construct questions where students produce fully labelled sketches of transformed sine, cosine, and tangent functions over a specified interval. Require identification and marking of: x-intercepts (exact), y-intercept, turning points (amplitude maxima/minima with exact coordinates), equations of vertical asymptotes for tangent, and endpoints of the interval. Include multi-function comparison tasks. Mark exactly the number of complete cycles fitting in the given interval. Label all turning points and x-intercepts with exact coordinates. For tangent, draw asymptotes as dashed vertical lines and label their equations. State the range of the function over the given interval.

subtopics.name: Modelling with Circular Functions
subtopics.technique_notes: Design real-world periodic modelling problems (tides, temperature, rotating machinery, sound waves) where students must: identify the amplitude and period from context, determine the phase shift from an initial condition, construct the equation $y = a\cos(b(x-h))+k$ or equivalent, and use the equation to answer predictive questions (e.g. find the first time a quantity exceeds a threshold). Convert period from context (e.g. '12 hours') to $b$ using $b = 2\pi/\text{period}$ for sine/cosine. Determine the midline $k = (\text{max} + \text{min})/2$ and amplitude $|a| = (\text{max} - \text{min})/2$. Use cosine for models where the starting point is a maximum/minimum; use sine for models starting at the midline. Always verify the equation at the known initial condition.

subtopics.name: Restricting Domains for Inverse Trig Functions
subtopics.technique_notes: Create questions where students identify the domain restriction required to make $\sin x$, $\cos x$, or $\tan x$ one-to-one, state the resulting inverse function with correct domain and range, evaluate inverse trig expressions for exact values, and sketch both the function (on its restricted domain) and its inverse showing reflection in $y = x$. Always state the domain and range of the inverse function explicitly. For evaluations, identify which quadrant the answer lies in based on the restricted domain, then use exact values. Do not use decimal approximations for tech-free items. When sketching, draw $y = x$ as a dashed reference line and clearly show the restricted domain endpoints.

subtopics.name: Logarithm Laws and Simplification
subtopics.technique_notes: Construct algebraic simplification tasks requiring fluent application of the logarithm laws: $\log_a(xy) = \log_a x + \log_a y$, $\log_a(x/y) = \log_a x - \log_a y$, $\log_a(x^n) = n\log_a x$, and $\log_a a = 1$. Include change-of-base problems and tasks requiring expression of a single logarithm as a combination, or vice versa. All work in $\log_e$ notation where appropriate. Use $\log_e$ notation (not $\ln$) throughout. Show each law application as a separate step. Verify that all arguments of logarithms remain positive after simplification. For change-of-base conversions, write $\log_a x = \frac{\log_e x}{\log_e a}$ explicitly before simplifying.

subtopics.name: Solving Exponential Equations
subtopics.technique_notes: Create multi-step problems where students solve exponential equations by taking $\log_e$ of both sides, applying logarithm laws, and isolating the unknown. Include equations with the unknown in the exponent on one or both sides (e.g. $a^{f(x)} = b^{g(x)}$), and equations reducible to a quadratic via substitution (e.g. $e^{2x} - 3e^x + 2 = 0$). Require exact solutions in $\log_e$ form. For equations of the form $a \cdot e^{kx} = b$, isolate the exponential first, then apply $\log_e$. For equations like $e^{2x} - 3e^x + 2 = 0$, use substitution $u = e^x$, factorise, solve for $u$, then recover $x = \log_e u$. Always reject negative solutions for $u$ (since $e^x > 0$). Express final answers as $x = \log_e(c)$ or $x = \frac{\log_e c}{k}$.

subtopics.name: Solving Logarithmic Equations
subtopics.technique_notes: Design problems where students solve logarithmic equations by applying $e^{(\cdot)}$ to both sides and using laws to simplify, working entirely in $\log_e$ notation. Require mandatory verification that all solutions satisfy the domain constraint (argument $> 0$). Include equations with logarithms on both sides, and equations requiring combination of logs before solving. Always combine logarithmic terms before exponentiating — never exponentiate term-by-term. After solving, substitute back into the original equation and verify each argument is strictly positive. Clearly state 'check: $[\text{argument}] > 0$ ✓' for each logarithm at the solution. Reject extraneous solutions with a written justification.

subtopics.name: Graph Features and Transformations of Exp/Log Functions
subtopics.technique_notes: Construct questions where students sketch transformed exponential and logarithmic functions, identify all key features (asymptotes, intercepts, domain, range), and describe the sequence of transformations from the base graph. Include reverse tasks: determining the equation of a transformed graph from given features. Cover $e^x$, $a^x$, $\log_e x$, $\log_a x$ and their transformations. Identify asymptote equations first, then intercepts, then sketch shape. For transformed exponentials, the horizontal asymptote shifts to $y = k$. For transformed logarithms, the vertical asymptote shifts to $x = h$. Describe transformations in numbered sequence using standard language and the form $y = af(b(x-h))+k$.

subtopics.name: Modelling Exponential Growth and Decay
subtopics.technique_notes: Design multi-step modelling problems where students: formulate an exponential equation from a worded context (e.g. population growth, radioactive decay, cooling), solve for unknown parameters using given conditions, use the model to make predictions, and verify solutions satisfy domain constraints. Include problems requiring $\log_e$ to solve for time or rate. Set up the equation system from given conditions clearly before solving. Use exact $\log_e$ expressions for $k$ and $t$ rather than decimal approximations. For half-life: set $y = A/2$ and solve for $t$. For doubling time: set $y = 2A$ and solve for $t$. Always interpret the answer in context (correct units and practical meaning).

subtopics.name: Standard Derivatives and Chain Rule
subtopics.technique_notes: Generate differentiation questions requiring students to apply standard derivative results and the chain rule to polynomial, exponential, logarithmic, and circular functions and their compositions. Include functions requiring multiple applications of the chain rule. Require exact derivative expressions in simplified form. Identify the outer and inner functions before applying the chain rule. Write the derivative in the form $f'(g(x)) \cdot g'(x)$ explicitly for multi-step chains. For $\frac{d}{dx}[a\log_e(bx)]$, note the inner factor $b$ cancels: the result is simply $a/x$. Simplify fully using algebra before stating the final answer.

subtopics.name: Product and Quotient Rules
subtopics.technique_notes: Construct differentiation questions requiring the product rule $\frac{d}{dx}[uv] = u'v + uv'$ and quotient rule $\frac{d}{dx}[u/v] = (u'v - uv')/v^2$. Include combinations with the chain rule and cases where simplification after differentiation is non-trivial. Require simplified exact forms for the derivative. Label $u$ and $v$ (and their derivatives $u'$, $v'$) explicitly before substituting. For quotient rule, expand the numerator and collect like terms. Prefer product rule over quotient rule where possible (e.g. write $x^{-2}$ rather than $1/x^2$). Factor the derivative to find zeros when stationary points are required.

subtopics.name: Stationary Points and Classification
subtopics.technique_notes: Design multi-part calculus problems where students: differentiate to find $f'(x)$, solve $f'(x) = 0$ for stationary points, construct a gradient table showing the sign of $f'(x)$ on intervals either side of each stationary point, and classify each point as a local maximum, local minimum, or stationary point of inflection. Require exact coordinates. The gradient table must show: a value of $x$ to the left of each stationary point, the stationary point itself, and a value to the right — with the corresponding sign of $f'(x)$ and an arrow indicating increasing ($\nearrow$) or decreasing ($\searrow$). State the classification explicitly in a conclusion sentence. Find $y$-coordinates by substituting $x$ back into $f(x)$, not $f'(x)$.

subtopics.name: Tangent and Normal Lines
subtopics.technique_notes: Create questions where students find equations of tangent and normal lines to curves at specified points. Require: evaluation of $f'(a)$ for the gradient of the tangent, use of $m_n = -1/f'(a)$ for the normal gradient, and expression of the line equation in the form $y - f(a) = m(x - a)$ or $y = mx + c$. Include cases where the tangent is horizontal or vertical, and reverse tasks finding the point given a tangent property. Compute $f(a)$ and $f'(a)$ before writing the line equation — show both values. Express the final equation in the form required by the question ($y = mx + c$ or general form). For reverse tasks (e.g. 'find $a$ such that the tangent has gradient $k$'), set $f'(a) = k$ and solve. Keep all values exact.

subtopics.name: Optimisation on Closed Intervals
subtopics.technique_notes: Design optimisation problems where students find the global maximum and minimum of a function on a closed interval $[a, b]$ by: finding all stationary points in the interior of the interval, evaluating $f$ at each stationary point and at both endpoints, and comparing all values. Include applied contexts (maximising area, minimising cost) with a constraint equation used to reduce to one variable. List all candidate points explicitly: stationary points found by solving $f'(x) = 0$, plus the two endpoints $x = a$ and $x = b$. Create a table of candidates and $f$-values and identify the global max/min clearly. For applied problems, state the constraint, substitute to eliminate one variable, state the domain of the resulting single-variable function, then optimise.

subtopics.name: Reading Derivative Graphs
subtopics.technique_notes: Present students with the graph of $f(x)$ and require them to sketch $f'(x)$, or vice versa — given the graph of $f'(x)$, sketch a possible graph of $f(x)$. Include questions identifying intervals of increase/decrease, locations of stationary points, and points of inflection from derivative graphs. Also include questions asking whether a given derivative graph is consistent with stated properties of $f$. When sketching $f'$ from $f$: identify where $f$ has stationary points (these become x-intercepts of $f'$), where $f$ is steepest (peaks/troughs of $f'$), and where $f$ changes concavity (stationary points of $f'$). When sketching $f$ from $f'$: identify sign changes of $f'$ (local extrema of $f$), then integrate qualitatively. Clearly mark all key $x$-values on both sketches.

subtopics.name: Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals
subtopics.technique_notes: Construct questions requiring students to find indefinite integrals of polynomial, exponential, reciprocal, circular, and power functions, always including the constant of integration $+c$. Include functions requiring algebraic manipulation before integration (e.g. expanding, splitting fractions). Require notation $\int f(x)\,dx = F(x) + c$ with the $dx$ term included. Always include $dx$ in the integral notation and $+c$ in the result. For sums and differences, integrate term by term. Simplify integrands algebraically before integrating — do not attempt to integrate an unexpanded product or unsimplified fraction. Express results in exact form.

subtopics.name: Antiderivatives of Composite Functions
subtopics.technique_notes: Create integration questions requiring the reverse chain rule to integrate composite functions of the form $f(ax + b)$. Cover exponential ($e^{ax+b}$), reciprocal ($1/(ax+b)$), circular ($\sin(ax+b)$, $\cos(ax+b)$), and power ($(ax+b)^n$) types. Include verification by differentiating the result. Identify the inner linear function $ax + b$ and the outer function type before integrating. Always divide by the coefficient $a$ of $x$. Verify by differentiating the result using the chain rule — the original integrand should be recovered exactly. For non-linear inner functions, a substitution method is needed (beyond this scope for tech-free).

subtopics.name: Definite Integrals and the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
subtopics.technique_notes: Design questions where students evaluate definite integrals $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$ using the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, showing bracket notation $[F(x)]_a^b = F(b) - F(a)$. Include functions requiring antidifferentiation of composite forms, and questions using properties of definite integrals (linearity, reversing limits, splitting at a point). Write $[F(x)]_a^b$ immediately after finding the antiderivative — never skip this notation. Substitute $b$ then $a$ separately and subtract: write $F(b) - F(a)$ explicitly. For exact results involving $\log_e$, leave in the form $\log_e(c)$ or $a\log_e(b) - c$. Do not add $+c$ to definite integrals.

subtopics.name: Area Under a Curve
subtopics.technique_notes: Create questions requiring students to calculate the area between a curve and the x-axis over a specified interval. Include cases where the function changes sign over the interval, requiring students to identify x-intercepts, split the integral at sign-change points, and add the absolute values of each signed integral. Require exact area results. Always sketch the function over the given interval (or reason about where $f(x) > 0$ and $f(x) < 0$) before setting up area integrals. State the sign of $f$ on each sub-interval. Calculate each sub-integral using bracket notation, then take absolute values and sum. Express the final area as an exact positive value.

subtopics.name: Area Between Curves
subtopics.technique_notes: Formulate questions where students find the area enclosed between two functions $f(x)$ and $g(x)$. Require: algebraic determination of intersection points, identification of which function is the upper curve on each sub-interval (using a test point or inequality), correct integral setup as $\int_a^b [f(x) - g(x)]\,dx$ (or split if curves intersect within the interval), and exact evaluation. Find intersections by solving $f(x) = g(x)$ algebraically. Test a value in each sub-interval to confirm which function is larger. Write the integrand as $[\text{upper} - \text{lower}]$ explicitly. If the integrand must always be non-negative (area is always positive), include a check. Evaluate each integral exactly using bracket notation and sum the results.

subtopics.name: Average Value and Rates of Change
subtopics.technique_notes: Design problems using the average value formula $\bar{f} = \frac{1}{b-a}\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$, including applied contexts (average temperature, average velocity). Also include questions where students recover a function $f(x)$ from a known rate $f'(x) = g(x)$ and a boundary condition $f(a) = k$, requiring integration followed by solving for the constant $c$. For average value: show the formula, substitute, evaluate the definite integral, then divide by the interval length. For recovery from a rate: write $f(x) = \int f'(x)\,dx = F(x) + c$, substitute the boundary condition explicitly (e.g. $F(a) + c = k$), solve for $c$, and state the complete function with its domain.

subtopics.name: Numerical Approximation: Trapezium Rule
subtopics.technique_notes: Construct questions where students apply the trapezium rule to approximate $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$ using $n$ strips: $\frac{h}{2}[f(x_0) + 2f(x_1) + \cdots + 2f(x_{n-1}) + f(x_n)]$ where $h = (b-a)/n$. Include tasks to determine whether the approximation is an overestimate or underestimate by linking to the concavity of $f$ on the interval. Require comparison with the exact value where computable. Compute $h$ and all $y_i$ values first in a table. Apply the formula carefully — middle values are doubled but endpoints are not. State whether the result is an over- or underestimate with a concavity justification: 'since $f''(x) > 0$ on $[a,b]$, the curve is concave up and the trapezium rule overestimates'. Express the approximation to the specified number of decimal places.

subtopics.name: Conditional Probability and Independence
subtopics.technique_notes: Design problems requiring students to calculate $\Pr(A|B) = \Pr(A \cap B)/\Pr(B)$ and test or apply the independence condition $\Pr(A \cap B) = \Pr(A)\Pr(B)$. Use two-way tables, Venn diagrams, and worded descriptions as information sources. Include problems where students must determine whether two events are independent, mutually exclusive, or neither, with algebraic justification. Use formal notation throughout: $\Pr(A|B)$, $\Pr(A \cap B)$, $\Pr(A \cup B)$. For two-way tables, extract $\Pr(A \cap B)$ directly as the cell count divided by total. Test independence by checking $\Pr(A \cap B) = \Pr(A) \times \Pr(B)$ numerically and stating a conclusion. Do not assume independence without verification.

subtopics.name: Probability Trees and Bayes' Theorem
subtopics.technique_notes: Create multi-stage probability problems solved using tree diagrams. Require students to label all branches with conditional probabilities, multiply along branches for joint probabilities, and add across branches for marginal probabilities. Include problems requiring Bayes' Theorem: $\Pr(A|B) = \Pr(B|A)\Pr(A)/\Pr(B)$, where $\Pr(B)$ is found via the law of total probability. Draw the tree with stages as columns and all branches clearly labelled with probabilities (not counts). Verify that branches at each node sum to 1. For Bayes' Theorem, compute $\Pr(B)$ using the law of total probability from the tree before dividing. Show all arithmetic explicitly — do not collapse steps.

subtopics.name: Sampling Distributions
subtopics.technique_notes: Develop conceptual and computational questions on the sample proportion $\hat{P}$ as a random variable. Require students to state the approximate distribution $\hat{P} \sim N(p, p(1-p)/n)$ for large $n$, calculate probabilities for $\hat{P}$ using this approximation, and explain why the normal approximation improves with increasing $n$. Include questions on the effect of sample size on spread. State the approximate distribution explicitly: '$\hat{P} \approx N\left(p, \frac{p(1-p)}{n}\right)$'. For probability calculations, standardise using $z = (\hat{p} - p)/\sqrt{p(1-p)/n}$ and use symmetry of the normal distribution where possible. For tech-free items, prefer problems where exact multiples of the standard deviation can be used.

subtopics.name: Confidence Intervals
subtopics.technique_notes: Create questions where students construct a 95% confidence interval for a population proportion $p$ using the formula $\hat{p} \pm 1.96\sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})/n}$, interpret the interval in context, and identify the effect of changing $n$ or the confidence level on the interval width. Include questions asking students to determine whether a hypothesised value of $p$ is plausible given an interval. Compute the margin of error $E = 1.96\sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})/n}$ as a separate step before stating the interval $[\hat{p}-E, \hat{p}+E]$. Round endpoints appropriately and state the interval clearly. For interpretation, use the phrase 'we are 95% confident that the true proportion lies between...' — never say 'there is a 95% probability that $p$ lies in this interval' (frequentist distinction).

subtopics.name: Solving for Sample Proportion or Sample Size
subtopics.technique_notes: Design algebraic inference questions where students: recover the sample proportion $\hat{p}$ from the endpoints of a given confidence interval (as the midpoint), calculate the margin of error from the interval width, and solve for the required sample size $n$ given a desired margin of error $E$ by inverting $E = 1.96\sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})/n}$. Include cases where $\hat{p}$ must first be recovered before solving for $n$. For recovering $\hat{p}$: compute the midpoint of the interval directly. For recovering $E$: compute half the interval width. For solving for $n$: square both sides of the margin-of-error formula, isolate $n$, and round up. Always state the rounding direction (ceiling) and explain why rounding down would violate the margin-of-error requirement.

subtopics.name: Probability Mass Functions
subtopics.technique_notes: Create tabular discrete probability distribution questions where students must: use the axiomatic property $\sum p(x) = 1$ to solve for an unknown probability constant $k$ (often requiring solution of a linear or simple quadratic equation in $k$), verify $p(x) \geq 0$ for all values after solving, and calculate specified probabilities by summing entries from the table. Set up the equation $\sum p(x) = 1$ explicitly by writing out each term. Solve for the unknown constant algebraically. Check $p(x) \geq 0$ for every value of $x$ in the table after substituting the solved constant — if any probability is negative, the value must be rejected. Present the distribution in a clearly labelled two-row table ($x$ and $p(x)$).

subtopics.name: Expected Value and Variance
subtopics.technique_notes: Design questions requiring manual calculation of $E(X) = \sum x \cdot p(x)$ and $\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2$ from tabular distributions (non-binomial). Include tasks finding $E(X^2) = \sum x^2 \cdot p(x)$ as an intermediate step, and problems asking students to interpret the expected value in context or compare distributions by their means and variances. Extend the probability table with columns for $x \cdot p(x)$ and $x^2 \cdot p(x)$ to organise the calculation. Sum each column to obtain $E(X)$ and $E(X^2)$ respectively. Apply $\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - \mu^2$ last. Keep all values as exact fractions throughout tech-free items.

subtopics.name: Binomial Distribution
subtopics.technique_notes: Create questions involving $X \sim \text{Bi}(n, p)$ where students: verify that Bernoulli trial conditions are met, calculate exact probabilities using $\Pr(X = x) = \binom{n}{x}p^x(1-p)^{n-x}$ for small $n$ (≤ 8), compute $E(X) = np$ and $\text{Var}(X) = np(1-p)$, and calculate cumulative probabilities by summing individual terms. Include tasks on how changing $n$ or $p$ affects the shape of the distribution. Define the variable clearly: 'Let $X$ be the number of successes in $n$ independent trials each with probability $p$ of success. Then $X \sim \text{Bi}(n,p)$.' Compute $\binom{n}{x}$ using the formula $n!/(x!(n-x)!)$ — show the evaluation step. For cumulative probabilities, list and sum individual terms explicitly. Keep $n \leq 8$ for tech-free items to maintain computational feasibility.

subtopics.name: Conditional Probability in Discrete Contexts
subtopics.technique_notes: Create questions requiring conditional probability calculations within discrete distributions — both tabular and binomial. Include problems of the form 'given that $X > a$, find $\Pr(X = k)$' requiring calculation of $\Pr(X = k \cap X > a)/\Pr(X > a)$ and problems where the condition restricts the sample space to a subset of outcomes. Identify $A \cap B$ explicitly before computing probabilities. For conditions like '$X > a$', compute $\Pr(X > a) = 1 - \Pr(X \leq a)$ or sum directly from the table. Express the conditional probability as a fraction in exact form. Verify the result is in $[0,1]$ and that the event $B$ has non-zero probability before proceeding.

subtopics.name: Constructing Valid PDFs
subtopics.technique_notes: Develop questions where students determine unknown constants in piecewise-defined PDFs by applying $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1$ and $f(x) \geq 0$. Require explicit statement of the support and $f(x) = 0$ elsewhere. Include cases requiring solution of linear and simple quadratic equations in the unknown constant. Verify the non-negativity condition after solving. Write the PDF piecewise: $f(x) = \{[\text{expression}], a \leq x \leq b; 0, \text{ otherwise}\}$. Set up $\int_a^b [\text{expression}]\,dx = 1$ and solve for $k$ algebraically. After solving, verify $f(x) \geq 0$ on $[a,b]$ by checking the expression is non-negative — if $k$ is negative or makes $f$ negative somewhere, reconsider the problem setup. State the final PDF in piecewise form.

subtopics.name: Calculating Probabilities from PDFs
subtopics.technique_notes: Create questions where students calculate $\Pr(a < X < b) = \int_a^b f(x)\,dx$ for continuous random variables, including cases where the interval extends beyond the support (requiring adjustment of integration limits to the support boundaries). Emphasise that $\Pr(X = a) = 0$ for continuous variables and include tasks exploiting this (e.g. $\Pr(X \geq a) = \Pr(X > a)$). Always identify the support before setting up limits of integration. If the given interval extends outside the support, clip the limits accordingly and note why. Use bracket notation for definite integrals. Express results as exact fractions or decimal values as specified. Note explicitly that $\Pr(X = k) = 0$ when the question involves a single value.

subtopics.name: Mean and Variance of Continuous Distributions
subtopics.technique_notes: Design questions where students compute the mean $\mu = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x f(x)\,dx$ and variance $\sigma^2 = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x^2 f(x)\,dx - \mu^2$ of a continuous random variable by integrating manually manageable integrands (low-degree polynomials, simple exponentials). Include tasks interpreting these values in context and comparing two distributions by their parameters. Compute $E(X)$ and $E(X^2)$ in separate clearly labelled steps. For polynomials, expand $x \cdot f(x)$ and $x^2 \cdot f(x)$ before integrating term by term. Apply $\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2$ last. Keep all values as exact fractions for tech-free items. Always integrate over the support only (not all of $\mathbb{R}$ when the PDF is piecewise).

subtopics.name: Median and Percentiles
subtopics.technique_notes: Create questions where students find the median $m$ of a continuous distribution by solving $\int_{-\infty}^{m} f(x)\,dx = 0.5$, and find a general percentile $k$ satisfying $\Pr(X \leq k) = c$ for a given $c \in (0,1)$. Include problems requiring algebraic solution of the resulting equation (polynomial, exponential) after evaluating the definite integral. Set up the integral $\int_{\text{lower support}}^{k} f(x)\,dx = c$ and evaluate using bracket notation, leaving $k$ as an unknown. Solve the resulting algebraic equation for $k$ and verify $k$ lies within the support. Check the answer by verifying $\Pr(X \leq k) \approx c$. For upper-tail probabilities, use $\Pr(X > k) = 1 - \Pr(X \leq k) = c$.

subtopics.name: Normal Distribution
subtopics.technique_notes: Design questions involving both the standard normal $N(0,1)$ and transformed normal $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$. Include tasks using symmetry ($\Pr(X > \mu) = 0.5$, $\Pr(\mu - k\sigma < X < \mu + k\sigma)$) for tech-free probability reasoning, and problems finding unknown percentiles $k$ from $\Pr(X > k) = c$ using the inverse normal. Include the effect of varying $\mu$ and $\sigma$ on the shape and position of the distribution. For tech-free items use exact multiples of $\sigma$: e.g. $\Pr(\mu - 2\sigma < X < \mu + \sigma)$ can be computed by splitting around $\mu$ and applying the 68-95-99.7% rule. For inverse normal problems, set $\Pr(Z < z^*) = 1 - c$ and identify $z^*$ from a table. Back-transform using $x = \mu + z^*\sigma$. Always state the distribution being used (e.g. '$Z \sim N(0,1)$') before computing.

subtopics.name: Additional Algebra and Number Systems
subtopics.technique_notes: Design questions involving the use of partial fractions to decompose rational expressions and the application of the factor theorem for higher-degree polynomials. Focus on identifying subsets of the complex and real number systems, including proofs of irrationality and the use of the Pigeonhole Principle in discrete contexts. Ensure problems require formal mathematical notation and logical step-by-step derivation to reflect tech-free rigor. For tech-free items, require algebraic manipulation by hand and exact answers. Use formal notation and step-by-step derivations to ensure rigor. Include problems that require proving properties of numbers or applying the Pigeonhole Principle in combinatorial contexts.

subtopics.name: Sequences and Series
subtopics.technique_notes: Construct problems focusing on arithmetic and geometric sequences, requiring students to find general terms, specific terms, and sums to $n$ terms. Include questions on infinite geometric series where the condition for convergence $|r| < 1$ must be identified and applied. Integrate applications of first-order linear recurrence relations, asking students to model real-world growth or decay scenarios and solve for steady-state solutions where applicable. For tech-free items, prefer sequences with hand-solvable terms and sums. Require explicit identification of convergence conditions for infinite series. Include real-world modelling problems that can be solved with recurrence relations, ensuring students derive and solve the relation algebraically.

subtopics.name: Reciprocals and Rational Functions
subtopics.technique_notes: Formulate tasks involving the sketching of reciprocal functions such as $y = 1/f(x)$ and rational functions including those with oblique asymptotes. Students should be required to identify vertical and horizontal asymptotes, intercepts, and the behavior of the function as $x$ approaches infinity. Questions should emphasize the relationship between the features of $f(x)$ and its reciprocal, particularly regarding stationary points and zeros. For tech-free items, require students to sketch functions by hand and identify key features without technological aid. Emphasize the algebraic manipulation of rational expressions and the interpretation of asymptotic behavior.

subtopics.name: Combinatorics and Matrices
subtopics.technique_notes: Develop combinatorial problems involving permutations and combinations with constraints, such as items that must be 'together' or 'separated.' In the matrix section, focus on matrix algebra, including the calculation of determinants and inverses for $2 \times 2$ matrices and their application in solving systems of linear equations. Include transformations in the plane where matrices are used to represent dilations, reflections, and rotations, requiring students to map coordinates accurately. For tech-free items, require students to perform matrix operations by hand and solve systems of equations algebraically.

subtopics.name: Trigonometric Functions and Identities
subtopics.technique_notes: Design questions requiring the use of compound angle, double angle, and Pythagorean identities to simplify expressions or solve complex trigonometric equations. Focus on the reciprocal functions (secant, cosecant, and cotangent) and the inverse trigonometric functions ($\arcsin$, $\arccos$, $\arctan$), including their restricted domains and ranges. Exam-style tasks should involve proving identities or using substitutions to evaluate integrals involving trigonometric terms. For tech-free items, require algebraic manipulation of trigonometric expressions and exact solutions. Include problems that test the ability to prove identities or evaluate integrals using trigonometric substitutions without technological aid.

subtopics.name: Proof
subtopics.technique_notes: Construct rigorous mathematical proof questions utilizing methods such as proof by induction, proof by contradiction, and proof by contrapositive. Focus on divisibility proofs, summation formulas, and inequality proofs, ensuring that students provide a clear 'Base Case,' 'Inductive Step,' and 'Conclusion' for induction. Questions should assess the ability to follow a logical chain of reasoning and use formal quantifiers like $\forall$ and $\exists$ correctly. For tech-free items, require students to write out full proofs by hand, emphasizing clarity and logical flow. Include problems that necessitate the use of formal quantifiers and require a structured approach to proof writing.

subtopics.name: Modulus
subtopics.technique_notes: Create problems involving the modulus function $|f(x)|$, requiring students to solve absolute value equations and inequalities algebraically and graphically. Questions should include sketching graphs of $y = |f(x)|$ and $y = f(|x|)$, emphasizing the piecewise nature of the function. Ensure students can identify 'sharp' points (cusps) where the derivative is undefined and use the definition $|x| = \sqrt{x^2}$ for algebraic manipulations. For tech-free items, require students to solve equations and inequalities by hand and sketch graphs without technological aid. Emphasize the piecewise definition of modulus functions and the identification of cusps in the graph.

subtopics.name: Algorithms and Graph Theory
subtopics.technique_notes: Formulate questions on graph theory concepts including vertices, edges, paths, and cycles, specifically focusing on Eulerian and Hamiltonian circuits. Include algorithmic tasks such as Dijkstra's algorithm for shortest paths or Prim's/Kruskal's algorithms for minimal spanning trees, requiring students to show their working through a table or step-by-step diagram. Problems should challenge students to analyze the efficiency or termination conditions of provided pseudo-code. For tech-free items, require students to perform algorithmic steps manually and analyze pseudo-code without technological aid. Include problems that test understanding of graph properties and the ability to apply algorithms to specific graph configurations.

subtopics.name: Graphing relations
subtopics.technique_notes: Design tasks involving the sketching of relations such as circles, ellipses, and hyperbolas, requiring students to identify centers, radii, vertices, and asymptotes. Questions should involve converting between general and standard forms using completing the square and determining the domain and range of the relation. Include problems where students must find the intersection points of a relation and a linear function or determine values of a parameter for which a line is tangent to a curve. For tech-free items, require students to sketch graphs by hand and perform algebraic manipulations without technological aid. Emphasize the conversion between general and standard forms of conic sections and the identification of key features such as centers, vertices, and asymptotes.

subtopics.name: Complex numbers
subtopics.technique_notes: Construct questions involving complex numbers in Cartesian, polar, and exponential forms, requiring students to perform operations and convert between forms using $z = r\text{cis}(\theta)$. Focus on the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra, solving polynomial equations with complex roots, and the geometric representation of sets in the complex plane (loci) such as circles and perpendicular bisectors. Exam-style tasks should include the use of De Moivre's Theorem to find powers and roots of complex numbers. For tech-free items, require students to perform calculations with complex numbers by hand and sketch their representations in the complex plane without technological aid.

subtopics.name: Transformations and Vectors in the plane
subtopics.technique_notes: Develop problems using vector notation ($u = xi + yj$) to solve geometric problems in the plane, including the dot product for finding angles between vectors and projections. Include questions on vector equations of lines and the use of vectors to prove geometric theorems. Transformations should be explored through vector addition and scalar multiplication, as well as matrix-vector multiplication for mapping shapes within the Cartesian coordinate system. For tech-free items, require students to perform vector operations by hand and solve geometric problems without technological aid.

topics.name: Chemistry
topics.examGuidance: Render every chemical formula/ionic species in LaTeX: $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{Fe}^{3+}$, $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$.

subtopics.name: Elements and the Periodic Table
subtopics.technique_notes: Create questions requiring the explanation of trends such as electronegativity, ionization energy, and atomic radius across periods and down groups using core charge and shielding effects. Include a sustainability focus by asking students to identify 'critical' elements used in modern technology and discuss the chemical or economic factors that lead to their endangerment. Students should justify the placement of elements in the periodic table based on their electronic configuration and resulting chemical properties. EXAM STYLE: - Questions should require explanation of trends using core charge and shielding concepts - Include sustainability context by asking about critical elements and their endangerment

subtopics.name: Molecular Structure: Lewis Structures, VSEPR Geometry, Polarity, and Intermolecular Forces
subtopics.technique_notes: Design tasks where students must draw Lewis (electron dot) structures for molecules and polyatomic ions to predict 3D shapes using VSEPR theory (e.g., tetrahedral, V-shaped). Questions should require students to link molecular symmetry and bond dipoles to overall polarity and then determine the dominant intermolecular forces (dispersion, dipole-dipole, or hydrogen bonding). Ensure assessments ask for the correlation between these forces and physical properties like boiling points and solubility. EXAM STYLE: - Tasks should require drawing Lewis structures and predicting VSEPR geometries - Questions should link molecular symmetry and bond dipoles to overall polarity - Assessments should correlate intermolecular forces with physical properties

subtopics.name: Metallic Bonding: Metallic Lattices and the Reactivity Series
subtopics.technique_notes: Formulate questions describing the metallic lattice as a sea of delocalized electrons surrounding fixed positive cations, requiring students to explain properties like malleability and electrical conductivity. Include problems involving the reactivity series of metals, where students predict the outcome of displacement reactions or the reaction of metals with water and acids. Tasks should challenge students to explain how alloying or heat treatment modifies the lattice structure to change the metal's properties. EXAM STYLE: - Questions should describe the metallic lattice and explain properties like malleability and conductivity - Include problems on the reactivity series to predict displacement reactions - Challenge students to explain how alloying or heat treatment modifies properties

subtopics.name: Ionic Chemistry: Ionic Bonding, Precipitation Reactions, and Solubility Tables
subtopics.technique_notes: Construct problems requiring students to write balanced chemical and full ionic equations for precipitation reactions using provided solubility tables. Questions should focus on the electrostatic attraction in ionic lattices and ask students to explain high melting points and brittleness based on lattice structure. Students must be able to identify spectator ions and predict the formation of a precipitate when two aqueous solutions are mixed. EXAM STYLE: - Problems should require writing balanced chemical and ionic equations for precipitation reactions - Questions should explain properties of ionic compounds based on lattice structure - Students must identify spectator ions and predict precipitate formation

subtopics.name: Chemical Quantities: Moles, Molar Mass, Percentage Composition, and Empirical/Molecular Formulas
subtopics.technique_notes: Develop stoichiometry problems requiring the use of $n=m/M$ and $n=N/N_A$ to calculate moles, masses, and numbers of particles. Questions should include determining empirical formulas from percentage composition by mass and then using molar mass to find the molecular formula. Ensure multi-step problems involve limiting reagent calculations and theoretical vs. actual yield percentages in a laboratory or industrial context. EXAM STYLE: - Problems should require calculations using the mole concept and molar mass - Include tasks on determining empirical and molecular formulas from percentage composition - Ensure multi-step problems involve limiting reagent and yield calculations

subtopics.name: Separation Techniques: Chromatography and Rf Value Identification
subtopics.technique_notes: Design questions centered on thin-layer or paper chromatography, requiring students to calculate $R_f$ values and interpret chromatograms based on the polarity of the stationary and mobile phases. Problems should ask students to predict the relative movement of components based on their affinity for each phase and explain how changing the solvent affects separation. Include applications of HPLC or GC, focusing on the interpretation of retention times and peak areas for qualitative and quantitative analysis. EXAM STYLE: - Questions should require calculation of Rf values and interpretation of chromatograms - Problems should predict movement of components based on polarity - Include applications of HPLC or GC with interpretation of retention times and peak areas

subtopics.name: Organic Classification: Alkanes, Alkenes, Alcohols, Carboxylic Acids, Haloalkanes, and IUPAC Naming
subtopics.technique_notes: Create tasks requiring the systematic IUPAC naming of organic molecules containing up to eight carbon atoms and various functional groups. Questions should involve drawing structural, semi-structural, and skeletal formulas and identifying isomers (structural and stereoisomers). Students must be able to classify alcohols as primary, secondary, or tertiary and predict the products of basic organic reactions like the addition of halogens to alkenes. EXAM STYLE: - Tasks should require systematic IUPAC naming of organic molecules - Questions should involve drawing various formula types and identifying isomers - Students must classify alcohols and predict products of basic organic reactions

subtopics.name: Polymer Chemistry: Addition and Condensation Polymerisation, Plastics, and Recycling
subtopics.technique_notes: Formulate questions on the formation of polymers from monomers, distinguishing between addition (e.g., polyethene) and condensation (e.g., polyesters, polyamides) processes. Students should be required to draw repeating units and identify the functional groups involved in the linkage. Include a discussion on the properties of thermoplastics versus thermosetting polymers and the environmental impact of plastic waste, focusing on the chemistry of recycling codes. EXAM STYLE: - Questions should distinguish between addition and condensation polymerisation - Tasks should require drawing repeating units and identifying functional groups - Include discussion on properties of thermoplastics vs thermosetting polymers and environmental impact of plastic waste

subtopics.name: Sustainability: Green Chemistry, Circular Economy, and Sustainable Development
subtopics.technique_notes: Develop problems that require students to evaluate chemical processes using the 12 principles of Green Chemistry, such as atom economy and the use of renewable feedstocks. Questions should focus on the transition from a linear 'take-make-dispose' model to a circular economy, emphasizing the reduction of waste and environmental footprint. Students must justify the choice of specific catalysts or solvents based on their sustainability and safety profiles. EXAM STYLE: - Problems should require evaluation of chemical processes using Green Chemistry principles - Questions should focus on the transition to a circular economy and reduction of waste - Students must justify the choice of catalysts or solvents based on sustainability and safety

subtopics.name: Water Chemistry: Hydrogen Bonding and Unique Physical Properties of Water
subtopics.technique_notes: Construct questions requiring the explanation of water's unique properties - such as high specific heat capacity, surface tension, and the density of ice - based on its extensive hydrogen bonding. Students should calculate energy changes using $q = mc\Delta T$ and explain the biological and environmental significance of water's ability to act as a universal solvent. Tasks should link the polarity of the water molecule to its interaction with various solutes through ion-dipole or hydrogen-bond forces. EXAM STYLE: - Questions should require explanation of water's unique properties based on hydrogen bonding - Tasks should include calculations of energy changes using q = mcΔT - Questions should link water's polarity to its solvent properties and interactions with solutes

subtopics.name: Acid-Base Chemistry: Bronsted-Lowry Theory, pH, Neutralisation, and Applications
subtopics.technique_notes: Design problems based on the Bronsted-Lowry theory of proton transfer, requiring students to identify conjugate acid-base pairs in various reactions. Questions should involve calculating $\text{pH}$, $[H^+]$, and $[OH^-]$ for strong acids and bases, as well as writing balanced equations for neutralization reactions. Include applications of buffers and the role of indicators in signaling the equivalence point of a reaction. EXAM STYLE: - Problems should require identification of conjugate acid-base pairs - Questions should involve pH calculations for strong acids and bases - Include tasks on writing balanced equations for neutralization reactions and applications of buffers and indicators

subtopics.name: Redox Chemistry: Electron Transfer, Half-Equations, Displacement, and Corrosion
subtopics.technique_notes: Formulate redox problems where students must assign oxidation numbers to identify the species being oxidized and reduced. Tasks should involve writing and balancing complex half-equations (in acidic or basic conditions) and combining them into a full ionic equation. Include scenarios involving the electrochemical series to predict the spontaneity of displacement reactions and the chemistry of corrosion prevention, such as sacrificial anodes or galvanizing. EXAM STYLE: - Problems should require assignment of oxidation numbers - Questions should involve writing and balancing half-equations - Include scenarios involving the electrochemical series and corrosion prevention

subtopics.name: Solutions: Concentration Units and Solubility Relationships
subtopics.technique_notes: Create questions involving the calculation of concentration in various units, including mol/L ($M$), g/L, ppm, and $\% v/v$ or $\% w/w$. Students should be required to interpret solubility curves to determine if a solution is saturated, unsaturated, or supersaturated at a given temperature. Include dilution problems using $C_1V_1 = C_2V_2$ and ask students to explain the process of dissolving at a molecular level, focusing on the breaking and forming of intermolecular forces. EXAM STYLE: - Questions should involve calculations of concentration in various units - Tasks should require interpretation of solubility curves - Include dilution problems and explanations of dissolution at the molecular level

subtopics.name: Volumetric Analysis: Acid-Base Titration, Standard Solutions, and Indicators
subtopics.technique_notes: Design multi-step stoichiometry problems based on titration data, requiring students to calculate the concentration of an unknown analyte. Questions should focus on the preparation of a primary standard solution, the selection of appropriate indicators based on $pK_a$, and the identification of concordant titers. Students must explain the difference between the equivalence point and the end point and identify potential sources of systematic or random error in the procedure. EXAM STYLE: - Problems should require multi-step calculations based on titration data - Questions should focus on preparation of primary standard solutions and selection of indicators - Include tasks on identifying concordant titers and explaining equivalence vs end point, as well as error analysis

subtopics.name: Gas Chemistry: Ideal Gas Equation and Greenhouse Gases
subtopics.technique_notes: Construct problems using the ideal gas equation $PV = nRT$ and molar volume at SLC to calculate the properties of gases involved in chemical reactions. Questions should also address the greenhouse effect, requiring students to explain how certain gas molecules absorb infrared radiation based on their molecular vibrations. Students should be able to discuss the environmental impact of increased concentrations of $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{CH}_4$, and $\text{N}_2\text{O}$ in the atmosphere. EXAM STYLE: - Problems should require calculations using the ideal gas equation and molar volume at SLC - Questions should address the greenhouse effect and molecular vibrations - Include discussion on the environmental impact of greenhouse gases

subtopics.name: Analytical Techniques: Electrical Conductivity, Stoichiometry, and Colorimetry/UV-Vis Spectroscopy
subtopics.technique_notes: Formulate tasks where students interpret data from analytical instruments to determine the concentration or identity of a sample. Questions should focus on the use of calibration curves in colorimetry and UV-Vis spectroscopy to determine unknown concentrations based on absorbance. Students must also be able to use gravimetric stoichiometry to calculate the mass of a precipitate and relate electrical conductivity to the concentration of ions present in a solution. EXAM STYLE: - Tasks should require interpretation of data from analytical instruments - Questions should focus on calibration curves for colorimetry and UV-Vis spectroscopy - Include problems on gravimetric stoichiometry and relating electrical conductivity to ion concentration

subtopics.name: Energy from Fuels: Combustion and Sustainability
subtopics.technique_notes: Develop tasks comparing the energy outputs and environmental impacts of fossil fuels versus biofuels. Questions should require calculating enthalpy changes using $\Delta H = q/n$ and writing balanced thermochemical equations for combustion. Evaluate fuel sustainability using green chemistry principles like atom economy and renewable feedstocks. EXAM STYLE: - Tasks should compare energy outputs and environmental impacts of fossil fuels vs biofuels - Questions should require calculations of enthalpy changes and writing thermochemical equations - Include evaluation of fuel sustainability using green chemistry principles

subtopics.name: Electrochemical Cells: Galvanic and Fuel Cells
subtopics.technique_notes: Design problems requiring students to predict cell voltage and reaction spontaneity using the electrochemical series. Tasks should involve drawing and labeling galvanic and fuel cells, identifying the anode/cathode, electron flow, and ion movement. Questions must address the operating principles of fuel cells, including the use of porous electrodes to increase efficiency. EXAM STYLE: - Problems should require prediction of cell voltage and reaction spontaneity using the electrochemical series - Tasks should involve drawing and labeling galvanic and fuel cells, identifying components and flows - Questions must address operating principles of fuel cells, including porous electrodes

subtopics.name: Chemical Equilibrium and Le Chatelier’s Principle
subtopics.technique_notes: Formulate questions requiring the calculation of equilibrium constants ($K_c$) and reaction quotients ($Q$). Students must use Le Chatelier’s principle to predict shifts in equilibrium systems in response to changes in temperature, concentration, or pressure. Include tasks that link these shifts to the yield of industrial processes. EXAM STYLE: - Questions should require calculations of equilibrium constants and reaction quotients - Tasks should use Le Chatelier's principle to predict shifts in equilibrium - Include problems linking equilibrium shifts to industrial process yields

subtopics.name: Electrolysis and Faraday’s Laws
subtopics.technique_notes: Construct problems requiring the application of Faraday’s Laws to calculate the mass of product or time required for electrolysis. Questions should involve identifying the products of electrolysis in both molten and aqueous systems using the electrochemical series and evaluating the sustainability of these processes. EXAM STYLE: - Problems should require application of Faraday's Laws to calculate mass of product or time for electrolysis - Questions should involve identifying products of electrolysis in molten and aqueous systems - Include evaluation of the sustainability of electrolysis processes

subtopics.name: Food Chemistry: Metabolism and Energy Value
subtopics.technique_notes: Design tasks focused on the chemical structures and metabolism of carbohydrates, proteins, and fats. Questions should involve writing equations for the hydrolysis of condensation polymers (e.g., starch, proteins) and calculating the energy content of food using calorimetry and specified energy values for macronutrients. EXAM STYLE: - Tasks should focus on chemical structures and metabolism of carbohydrates, proteins, and fats - Questions should involve writing equations for hydrolysis of condensation polymers - Include calculations of energy content using calorimetry and specified energy values for macronutrients

subtopics.name: Instrumental Analysis of Organic Compounds
subtopics.technique_notes: Create problems requiring the deduction of organic structures from analytical data, including IR, NMR ($^1H$ and $^{13}C$), and mass spectrometry. Students must identify functional groups from IR absorption bands and determine carbon environments and hydrogen splitting patterns from NMR spectra. EXAM STYLE: - Problems should require deduction of organic structures from IR, NMR, and mass spectrometry data - Questions must identify functional groups from IR absorption bands - Tasks should determine carbon environments and hydrogen splitting patterns from NMR spectra

subtopics.name: Medicinal Chemistry and Extraction
subtopics.technique_notes: Formulate questions on how medicines function at a molecular level, such as enzyme inhibition or receptor interactions. Include tasks requiring students to design or evaluate processes for the extraction and purification of natural medicines using solvent extraction or distillation. EXAM STYLE: - Questions should focus on the molecular action of medicines, such as enzyme inhibition or receptor interactions - Tasks should require design or evaluation of extraction and purification processes for natural medicines using solvent extraction or distillation

topics.name: Physical Education
topics.examGuidance:
VCAA PHYSICAL EDUCATION EXAM QUESTION GENERATION - COMPREHENSIVE INSTRUCTIONS
WRITING STYLE FOR PHYSICAL EDUCATION:
- Do NOT use mathematical equations, derivations, or formula-heavy solutions.
- Write all worked solutions and exemplar responses in prose - paragraphs and bullet points.
- Simple named formulas are acceptable where the Study Design requires them (e.g. 'Fitt's principle', '$	ext{VO}_2$ max', '1RM', 'F = ma') - mention by name and explain application in words. Do NOT rearrange, derive, or chain equations.
- Focus on qualitative analysis, evaluation, and justification rather than calculation.

PE EXAM QUESTION TYPES & COGNITIVE DEMANDS:

SHORT-ANSWER QUESTIONS (1-2 marks) - RECALL/COMPREHENSION
|- Verbs: Define, identify, list, state, describe (basic), explain (simple)
|- Example: "Define the term 'discrete motor skill'."
`- Expected response: 1-2 sentences with accurate definition

EXTENDED-RESPONSE QUESTIONS (2-6 marks) - APPLICATION/ANALYSIS
|- Verbs: Explain (with evidence), apply, demonstrate, analyze, evaluate, justify
|- Example: "Explain how the ATP-CP system is particularly suited to high-intensity, short-duration activities. Refer to its rate of ATP regeneration and the role of phosphocreatine in your response."
`- Expected response: 3-4 detailed sentences or structured paragraphs

APPLICATION/SCENARIO QUESTIONS (3-8 marks) - SYNTHESIS/INTEGRATION
|- Verbs: Design, evaluate, critique, create, analyze (multi-component), justify (complex)
|- Example: "A netball player is training to improve her vertical jump for shooting. Design a 4-week periodized training program that includes appropriate training methods and progression. Justify your choice of methods with reference to the physiological demands of jumping, energy systems used, and skill acquisition principles."
`- Expected response: Structured program with detailed scientific justification

DATA INTERPRETATION QUESTIONS (2-5 marks) - OBSERVATION/INFERENCE/APPLICATION
|- Verbs: Interpret, analyze, identify (trends), explain (what data shows)
|- Example: "The graph shows a swimmer's lactate levels during a 400 m freestyle race. (a) Identify the point(s) where the anaerobic threshold was exceeded. (b) Explain what this suggests about energy system contribution. (c) Recommend a training method to improve lactate tolerance."
`- Expected response: Observation -> explanation -> application progression

COMPARISON QUESTIONS (2-6 marks) - DIFFERENTIATION/CONTRAST
|- Verbs: Compare (similarities AND differences), contrast, distinguish, evaluate (relative merit)
|- Example: "Compare the relative contribution of the aerobic and anaerobic glycolysis systems during (a) a 100 m sprint and (b) a 1500 m run. In your answer, consider rate of ATP yield, duration, and total ATP production."
`- Expected response: Structured comparison with specific references to both similarities and differences

AUTHENTICITY & CONTEXT REQUIREMENTS:
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

VCAA questions must be grounded in REALISTIC sports and training contexts.

COMMON AUSTRALIAN SPORTS:
AFL, cricket, rugby league, rugby union, netball, basketball, tennis, swimming, athletics (track & field), cycling, badminton, gymnastics, soccer (football)

REALISTIC SCENARIOS:
- Pre-season training and conditioning phases
- Return-to-play after injury / rehabilitation
- Peaking for competition (tapering strategies)
- Performance plateau problems and solutions
- Adapting training for different athlete levels (beginner, intermediate, elite)
- Managing recovery and fatigue in multi-competition seasons
- Technique refinement under fatigue

ATHLETE PERSPECTIVES:
- Scenarios should relate to experiences of VCE PE students (school athletes, club athletes, potential elite pathway athletes)
- Acknowledge recreational participation, competitive club level, and elite development
- Consider both individual sports (tennis, gymnastics, swimming) and team sports (netball, AFL, basketball, soccer)

QUALITY CHECKS BEFORE FINALIZING ANY QUESTION:

[OK] Aligned with Unit 3 or 4 key knowledge/skills from Study Design?
[OK] Requires higher-order thinking (application, analysis, synthesis)?
[OK] Context is realistic and relevant to VCE PE students?
[OK] Mark allocation matches cognitive demand appropriately?
[OK] Question is unambiguous and free of jargon confusion?
[OK] A clear, defensible marking rubric exists?
[OK] Question integrates multiple concepts where appropriate?
[OK] All terms are defined or contextually clear?
[OK] Question avoids leading or telegraphing the expected answer?
[OK] Expected response length is reasonable for marks allocated?
[OK] Question reflects the style and rigor of actual VCAA examinations?

subtopics.name: Movement Skill Classification: Fundamental, Sport-Specific, Open/Closed, Gross/Fine
subtopics.technique_notes: Design questions that require the classification of skills across multiple continuums simultaneously, such as a 'gross, closed, sport-specific skill.' Students must provide a justification for each classification by referencing environmental predictability and the size of the muscle groups involved. Exam-style tasks should use specific sport scenarios to test the student's ability to distinguish between open and closed environments, avoiding the common error of confusing environmental stability with the skill's complexity. EXAM STYLE: - Questions must require classification using MULTIPLE classification systems simultaneously - Test application: give a sport-specific scenario and ask student to justify classifications - Common errors to probe: confusing open/closed with predictable/unpredictable environments - VCAA pattern: define -> classify -> justify with sport example -> explain participation link

subtopics.name: Discrete, Serial, and Continuous Motor Skills
subtopics.technique_notes: Formulate problems focusing on the temporal boundaries of skills, requiring students to identify whether a movement has a distinct beginning and end or is repetitive in nature. Questions should ask for an explanation of how serial skills are formed by combining discrete elements into a coordinated sequence. Include coaching implications, such as why continuous skills are better suited for 'whole practice' while discrete skills may benefit from 'part practice' during the early stages of learning. EXAM STYLE: - Focus on temporal boundaries: clear beginning/end (discrete), arbitrary endpoints (continuous) - Serial skills: sequence of discrete skills combined into a coordinated routine - VCAA pattern: identify skill type -> explain temporal characteristics -> coaching implication - Link to practice scheduling: discrete suits part practice; continuous suits whole practice

subtopics.name: Stages of Learning: Cognitive, Associative, and Autonomous Stages
subtopics.technique_notes: Construct scenario-based questions where students must identify a performer's stage of learning based on described characteristics such as error frequency, consistency, and attentional focus. Responses must justify the identification with evidence from the scenario and recommend a coaching strategy tailored to that stage, such as high-frequency augmented feedback for the cognitive stage. Ensure questions probe the transition between stages, particularly the shift from external to internal feedback dependency. EXAM STYLE: - Test characteristics of each stage: error rate, consistency, attentional focus, feedback dependency - Scenario-based: describe a performer and ask student to identify stage with justification - VCAA pattern: identify stage -> justify with evidence -> recommend appropriate coaching strategy - Common errors: confusing associative with autonomous; not linking stage to feedback type

subtopics.name: Skill Acquisition Theories: Linear vs. Non-Linear Learning Models
subtopics.technique_notes: Develop comparative questions that ask students to contrast linear models, which focus on predictable stages and direct instruction, with non-linear models like the constraints-led approach. Tasks should require the application of these theories to a specific learner, identifying how individual, task, and environmental constraints influence the emergence of movement patterns. Students must justify which approach is more suitable for a given scenario based on the complexity of the skill and the learner's experience. EXAM STYLE: - Linear: stages of learning, direct instruction, predictable progression - Non-linear: constraints-led approach, dynamical systems theory, individual variability - VCAA pattern: compare theories -> apply to scenario -> justify which suits a given learner - Must reference individual, task, and environmental constraints for non-linear approaches

subtopics.name: Learning Approaches: Direct Instruction vs. Constraint-Based Methods
subtopics.technique_notes: Create problems that require defining and comparing direct instruction (coach-led, explicit) with constraint-based methods (game sense, implicit). Questions should ask for the advantages and disadvantages of each approach and a recommendation for a specific stage of learning, such as using direct instruction for beginners to establish a safe technical foundation. Students must explain how the manipulation of task constraints in a game-like environment facilitates problem-solving and skill transfer. EXAM STYLE: - Direct instruction: explicit teaching, coach-led, suitable for cognitive stage, predictable environments - Constraint-based: implicit learning, game sense, manipulation of constraints (task, individual, environment) - VCAA pattern: define approaches -> compare advantages/disadvantages -> recommend for specific scenario - Link to stages of learning: direct suits early stages; constraint-based suits associative/autonomous

subtopics.name: Practice Scheduling: Type (Whole/Part), Distribution (Massed/Distributed), and Variability (Blocked/Random)
subtopics.technique_notes: Design tasks requiring students to recommend and justify a practice schedule based on the complexity and organization of a skill.
